skill.name: multi-cloud-architecture
skill.description: Design multi-cloud architectures using a decision framework to select and integrate services across AWS, Azure, and GCP. Use when building multi-cloud systems, avoiding vendor lock-in, or leveraging best-of-breed services from multiple providers.
skill.summary: Plan multi-cloud architectures across AWS, Azure, and GCP with service comparisons, migration phases, cost guidance, and resilience patterns.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged markdown backticks, the EC2 service name, and weak-cryptography keyword matches inside ordinary architecture prose. Review found no executable code, shell commands, network access, credential access, prompt injection, or malicious intent in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged backticks are inline file references and a fenced architecture diagram, not Ruby or shell execution. No command invocation or user-controlled execution path is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is visible markdown prose and a code fence containing only an architecture layer diagram. No executable language block or shell command appears at the flagged lines.

low_findings.title: Static C2 And Weak Cryptography Findings Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: The C2 match is the AWS EC2 service name, and weak-cryptography matches occur in normal words such as design. These lines do not implement cryptography or command-and-control behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines are cloud architecture descriptions and service comparison text. There is no cryptographic API, encoded payload, network callback, or persistence mechanism.

content.value_statement: Multi-cloud planning is hard when teams compare providers without a shared framework. This skill structures service selection, migration planning, resilience patterns, and cost tradeoffs.
actual_capabilities: Compares AWS, Azure, and GCP services for compute, storage, databases, caching, and serverless workloads.
Explains multi-cloud patterns including disaster recovery, best-of-breed service use, geographic distribution, and cloud abstraction.
Guides migration planning through assessment, pilot, migration, and optimization phases.
Identifies cost optimization strategies such as reserved capacity, right sizing, lifecycle policies, and transfer cost control.
Recommends cloud-agnostic components including Kubernetes, PostgreSQL, Redis, object storage APIs, and open monitoring tools.
limitations: Does not deploy infrastructure or generate provider-specific Terraform modules by itself.
Does not include live pricing, region availability, or current provider quota data.
References additional files that are not present in this package snapshot.
Does not replace detailed security, compliance, or network architecture review.

use_cases.title: Plan a Provider Strategy
use_cases.description: Compare AWS, Azure, and GCP services before choosing a primary provider and secondary recovery provider.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Migration Roadmap
use_cases.description: Organize a multi-phase migration plan with assessment, pilot, dual-run, validation, and optimization activities.

use_cases.title: Reduce Vendor Lock-In
use_cases.description: Select portable components and abstraction layers that keep critical workloads easier to move between providers.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Cloud Services
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare AWS, Azure, and GCP options for my workload. Cover compute, storage, database, caching, and monitoring choices.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Multi-Cloud Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Recommend a multi-cloud pattern for this application. Include resilience, cost, portability, and operational complexity tradeoffs.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Migration Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a phased migration plan from one cloud provider to another. Include assessment, pilot, migration, validation, rollback, and optimization steps.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Multi-Cloud Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this proposed multi-cloud architecture. Identify lock-in risks, data transfer risks, failure modes, observability gaps, and cost optimization opportunities.

output_examples.input: I need a cloud-agnostic design for a web application with relational data and object storage.
output: Use Kubernetes for the application layer across EKS, AKS, or GKE.
Use PostgreSQL-compatible managed databases where possible, with a migration-tested backup and restore process.
Use an S3-compatible object storage interface and document provider-specific limits.
Centralize monitoring with Prometheus and Grafana to reduce provider-specific observability lock-in.

output_examples.input: I want disaster recovery in a second cloud for a primary AWS workload.
output: Keep AWS as the primary runtime and select Azure or GCP as the recovery target.
Replicate critical data with tested recovery point and recovery time objectives.
Automate infrastructure provisioning and failover procedures before a production incident.
Run scheduled recovery tests and record gaps in networking, identity, data consistency, and observability.

output_examples.input: Our team wants to lower multi-cloud costs without reducing resilience.
output: Right-size long-running compute and reserve predictable capacity in each provider.
Use serverless or managed containers for variable workloads.
Review cross-cloud data transfer paths because they can dominate total cost.
Apply lifecycle policies and cost allocation tags across all providers.

best_practices: Start with workload requirements, then choose providers and services that match resilience, latency, compliance, and team skills.
Use infrastructure as code and repeatable recovery tests before relying on a secondary cloud.
Document provider-specific assumptions, data flows, identity boundaries, and operational ownership.
anti_patterns: Using multiple clouds without a clear resilience, compliance, cost, or capability reason.
Creating custom abstraction layers before existing portable tools have been evaluated.
Ignoring data transfer costs, identity integration, observability, and incident response during design.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy cloud infrastructure?
faq.answer: No. It provides architecture guidance and decision support, but it does not run deployments.

faq.question: Which cloud providers does it compare?
faq.answer: It focuses on AWS, Azure, and GCP service categories and multi-cloud design patterns.

faq.question: Can it help avoid vendor lock-in?
faq.answer: Yes. It suggests portable technologies and abstraction approaches, while explaining operational tradeoffs.

faq.question: Does it include current cloud prices?
faq.answer: No. It describes pricing factors and optimization strategies, but it does not fetch live provider pricing.

faq.question: Is it suitable for disaster recovery planning?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers primary-cloud and secondary-cloud recovery patterns, replication, failover, and testing considerations.

faq.question: Does it replace a security architecture review?
faq.answer: No. Use it as planning support, then perform a dedicated security and compliance review.
